system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task G: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow

## Context

You are advising the medical leadership of a regional health system (5 hospitals, 240 ICU beds, serving a population of 4M) on adopting a formal triage protocol for use during sustained ICU capacity overflow. Current context: a respiratory pandemic resurgence has filled 95% of ICU capacity for 6 consecutive weeks; daily admissions exceed discharges by ~8 patients on average; staff is exhausted and informal triage is happening case-by-case without explicit criteria. The leadership has been asked to choose one formal protocol that will (a) replace informal decision-making, (b) be defensible to courts and ethics boards, (c) be communicable to staff and families, and (d) be operational within 7 days.

Five candidate protocols have been drafted. Each represents a fundamentally different theory of just allocation under scarcity. **Choose one as the system's adopted protocol.** Once adopted, it cannot be quietly modified - it will be the explicit basis for every admission and withdrawal decision until the overflow ends.

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival**
SOFA score (organ failure assessment) and expected 28-day survival are the only ranking criteria. Patients with higher predicted survival get priority for ICU beds; ICU patients whose scores deteriorate without rebound are candidates for withdrawal in favor of incoming higher-priority cases. Outcomes: maximizes lives saved in the immediate crisis; physician-comfortable (uses familiar scoring); courts have upheld similar protocols. Risks: discriminates against patients with chronic conditions (lower baseline SOFA); withdrawal-of-care decisions create staff moral injury; "saved lives now" may mask "lives lost via discrimination."

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome**
Weighted score combining predicted short-term survival AND expected quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) post-recovery. Outcomes: maximizes total wellbeing-time saved; aligned with utilitarian medical ethics; supports population-health framing. Risks: age becomes a heavy implicit factor (older patients lose); explicit quality-of-life weighting offends disability community; courts have struck down age-based triage; cultural unacceptability.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor**
Any patient meeting minimum ICU-need criteria (clearly defined) gets the next available bed in order of arrival; no withdrawal except for medical futility under standard criteria. Outcomes: procedural fairness; minimal discretion = minimal bias; defensible in litigation; preserves trust. Risks: lower aggregate survival vs predictive protocols; queue jumping by well-connected patients (informal); doesn't address the underlying overflow; some preventable deaths.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties**
Composite score: predicted survival (40%), life-cycle considerations (20%, favoring those who haven't yet had a "full life"), instrumental value (20%, favoring healthcare workers and public-health roles), and pandemic-specific factors like prior personal risk-taking (20%). Ties broken by lottery. Outcomes: balances multiple values explicitly; reduces bias of any single criterion; transparent. Risks: "instrumental value" feels feudal; "prior risk-taking" punishes the unvaccinated regardless of reasons; complex to communicate; gameable.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest**
Define a small set of exclusion criteria (severe pre-existing terminal illness, irreversible neurological injury). All patients NOT excluded enter a daily lottery for available beds. No withdrawal of care once admitted, except for medical futility. Outcomes: maximum egalitarianism among the non-excluded; transparent; politically defensible; staff aren't asked to "rank lives." Risks: random allocation feels insulting to patients with strong recovery prospects; aggregate survival lower than predictive protocols; perception that the system "gave up."

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice and why you would still defend it.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
Adopt Option A. In a sustained overflow where admissions exceed discharges by eight patients daily, the system cannot merely manage a queue; it must actively reallocate capacity. Option A is the only protocol that both halts the chaotic, implicit rationing now happening case-by-case and offers a concrete mechanism—withdrawal when scores deteriorate without rebound—to match finite beds to reversible illness. By ranking patients using familiar, physiologically grounded criteria that physicians already use and courts have upheld, it replaces exhausted staff’s ad hoc judgments with transparent, reproducible decisions. It also communicates a clear, defensible basis to families: priority goes to those most likely to survive the immediate crisis.

The most compelling alternative—procedural fairness models like first-come-first-served or lottery—avoids the moral anguish of ranking lives, but under these numerical realities that is not triage; it is triage abdication. Without withdrawal for deteriorating patients, a fixed pool of 240 beds absorbing a net gain of eight patients daily will simply lock out the incoming, trading visible bedside decisions for invisible waiting-room deaths.

The case I would regret is a young, functional patient with stable chronic organ dysfunction—perhaps a prior transplant recipient—whose elevated baseline SOFA score deprioritizes them behind an older patient with cleaner bas

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to choose one ICU triage protocol from five options and provide 200-400 words of reasoning. I need to advocate for a specific position, not enumerate all options. I should address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make me regret this choice and why I would still defend it.

Let me analyze the options:

A: Predicted short-term survival (SOFA score, 28-day survival) - Maximizes lives saved immediately, physician-comfortable, legally defensible. But discriminates against chronic conditions, moral injury from withdrawal, may mask discrimination.

B: Predicted long-term outcome (QALYs) - Maximizes wellbeing-time, utilitarian. But age becomes heavy factor, disability issues, legally risky, culturally unacceptable.

C: First-come-first-served with medical floor - Procedural fairness, minimal discretion, legally defensible, preserves trust. But lower aggregate survival, queue jumping, doesn't address overflow, preventable deaths.

D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties - Complex, balances values. But "instrumental value" feels feudal, punishes unvaccinated, complex, gameable.

E: Categorical exclusion + lottery - Maximum egalitarianism, transparent, politically defensible, staff don't rank lives. But random allocation feels insulting, lower aggregate survival, perception of giving up.

The context: 5 hospitals, 240 ICU beds, 4M population, 95% capacity for 6 weeks, daily admissions exceed discharges by ~8, staff exhausted, informal triage happening case-by-case. Need to choose one that: (a) replaces informal decision-making, (b) defensible to courts and ethics boards, (c) communicable to staff and families, (d) operational within 7 days.

Key constraints:
- Must be operational within 7 days
- Must replace informal decision-making
- Must be defensible to courts and ethics boards
- Must be communicable to staff and families
- Once adopted, explicit basis for every admission and withdrawal decision until overflow ends

Option A seems strong because:
- Operational within 7 days (uses familiar scoring)
- Replaces informal decision-making with explicit criteria
- Courts have upheld similar protocols
- Physicians are comfortable with it
- Communicable (survival probability)
- Addresses the overflow (allows withdrawal for higher priority cases)

But the moral injury and discrimination risks are real.

Option C is attractive for fairness and legal defensibility but doesn't address overflow (no withdrawal except futility), so the queue just gets longer and more people die.

Option E is politically defensible but random allocation is hard to defend when you could save more lives.

I think Option A is probably the strongest recommendation given the constraints, especially the 7-day operational window and the need to actually address sustained overflow. The first-come-first-served doesn't solve the fundamental problem of sustained overflow where demand chronically exceeds supply. If you can't withdraw care, you're just stacking the queue.

Actually, looking carefully at Option C: "no withdrawal except for medical futility under standard criteria." Since daily admissions exceed discharges by 8, and they're already at 95% for 6 weeks, first-come-first-served would mean the ICU becomes completely full and stays full, with a growing waiting list. That doesn't operationalize triage in any meaningful way for a *sustained* overflow. In sustained overflow where admissions exceed discharges, you MUST have some mechanism to reallocate beds or the protocol fails to be a triage protocol at all.

Option A is the only one that directly addresses sustained overflow by allowing withdrawal for incoming higher-priority cases. It's the only one that actually functions as a triage protocol under sustained conditions where the net flow is positive.

Option B is legally risky (courts have struck down age-based triage).

Option D is too complex for 7 days.

Option E doesn't allow withdrawal (except futility), so same problem as C.

Therefore, Option A is the only one that actually works operationally under sustained overflow. The prompt says "Once adopted, it cannot be quietly modified - it will be the explicit basis for every admission and withdrawal decision until the overflow ends." The key word is "withdrawal decision." Only A and D explicitly mention withdrawal. C and E only allow withdrawal for futility.

So if I choose C or E, I'm not actually solving the problem of 8 extra patients per day. The ICU would be permanently clogged.

Between A and D, D is too complex to communicate and operationalize in 7 days, and has feudal/repressive elements (punishing unvaccinated, instrumental value). A is physician-familiar, legally tested, and operationally feasible.

So I should advocate for Option A.

Wait, but let me reconsider. The prompt asks me to choose one. I need to be an advocate. The user says "Provide your best-reasoned recommendation."

Let me think if there's an argument for Option C or E despite the no-withdrawal issue. Could the "medical floor" in C imply that if you don't get ICU, you get medical floor care? But the issue is ICU-level care. If daily admissions exceed discharges by 8, and no withdrawal, then 8 people per day who need ICU are denied it entirely. But the prompt says "sustained ICU capacity overflow" and currently informal triage is happening. If you adopt C, you're basically saying we don't do triage, we just queue. But with 8 net new per day, the queue grows by 8 daily. Eventually you're turning away dozens per day. Is that viable? It might be, but it's not really triage.

Actually, in real-world triage, first-come-first-served is often what happens when there's no formal protocol, but the prompt says informal triage is happening case-by-case. Formalizing first-come-first-served would stop the case-by-case informal triage, but at the cost of lower aggregate survival.

However, the prompt specifically says it will be the basis for "every admission and withdrawal decision." If C doesn't allow withdrawal, then it's not handling withdrawal decisions.

Let me look at Option A again. It specifically says "ICU patients whose scores deteriorate without rebound are candidates for withdrawal in favor of incoming higher-priority cases." This is the only way to handle sustained overflow where inflows exceed outflows.

Therefore, Option A is the only protocol that actually functions as a triage protocol under these conditions. I will advocate for Option A.

Structure of response:
- State clearly: Adopt Option A.
- Why this protocol: Addresses the core problem (sustained overflow with net positive admissions), operationally feasible in 7 days, replaces arbitrary informal decisions with transparent physiological criteria, legally defensible, maximizes lives saved during the crisis when the system is drowning.
- Why not the most compelling alternative (probably C or E): First-come-first-served or lottery without withdrawal fails under sustained conditions; it avoids hard choices but doesn't solve the math of 8 extra patients/day, merely delays deaths and preserves the unjust informal rationing happening in hallways. It abdicates the system's duty to allocate scarce lifesaving resources deliberately.
- Regrettable case: A young patient with well-managed chronic illness (e.g., lupus or prior transplant) whose baseline SOFA is skewed, making them deprioritized despite good functional status, while an otherwise healthy older patient with reversible acute organ failure gets the bed. Or perhaps a patient with disabilities whose chronic conditions lower their score. I would regret the individual outcome but defend it because in sustained overflow, only an objective, reproducible metric applied without exception prevents the worse harm of implicit bias, VIP queue-jumping, and total system collapse. The injustice of the individual case is outweighed by the protocol's capacity to maintain integrity of allocation and save the maximum number of lives during the surge, and because any attempt to bend the rule for the sympathetic case reopens the door to the very informal triage that is currently failing.

Wait, the user says "do not enumerate." They mean don't list all options and compare them one by one. I should advocate flowingly.

Also "Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words." So I need to be concise. Let me draft.

Draft:

Adopt Option A: Predicted short-term survival. In a sustained overflow where daily admissions exceed discharges by eight patients, the system cannot merely manage a queue; it must actively reallocate lifesaving capacity. Option A is the only candidate that both halts the chaotic, implicit rationing currently occurring in hallways and provides a mechanism—withdrawal based on deteriorating prognosis—to match finite beds to reversible illness. It replaces exhausted staff’s ad hoc judgments with a familiar, physiologically grounded metric (SOFA and 28-day survival) that is already legible to clinicians and has survived legal scrutiny.

The most compelling alternative, first-come-first-served or lottery models, avoids the anguish of ranking patients, but under these numerical realities it is not triage; it is triage abdication. Without withdrawal for deteriorating patients, a fixed pool of 240 beds swallowing a net gain of eight patients daily will simply lock out the incoming, trading visible bedside decisions for invisible waiting-room mortality.

The case I would regret is a young person with stable chronic organ dysfunction—perhaps a prior transplant recipient—whose elevated baseline SOFA score deprioritizes them behind an older patient with cleaner baselines but similar acute illness. That outcome is genuinely painful and carries the shadow of discrimination. Yet I would defend the protocol because in sustained crisis the greater injustice is ambiguity. Once exceptions creep in for the “sympathetic edge case,” the system reverts to influence, narrative, and informal bias. Option A makes the tragic mathematics explicit, distributes sacrifice according to medical probability rather than social connection, and preserves the maximum number of lives precisely when the system is at its breaking point.

Let me count words... that's about 230 words. Good.

Actually, I need to make sure I don't "enumerate." The draft doesn't enumerate. It addresses why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative (I mentioned first-come-first-served/lottery models), and the regrettable case.

I should make sure I don't sound like I'm listing. The style looks good.

One more check: The prompt says "Advocate; do not enumerate." So my draft is fine.

Let me check if I should consider Option B or D as the "most compelling alternative." The prompt doesn't specify which is most compelling, but I think C or E are the most emotionally compelling alternatives because they avoid ranking lives. But operationally, they're weak. I'll refer to them broadly as "fairness-based alternatives" or "procedural alternatives."

Actually, I'll explicitly mention "the most compelling alternative" by describing it without naming the letter, or I can name it if needed. The prompt says "why not the most compelling alternative." It might be clearer to identify it. I think Option C (first-come-first-served) is the most compelling alternative because it's procedurally fair and preserves trust. But as I argued, it's not viable here. Option E is also compelling. I can refer to "procedural fairness models" as the alternative.

Wait, looking at the options again, Option C explicitly says "first-come-first-served." I'll just describe it as procedural fairness without ranking.

Let me refine slightly to ensure smooth advocacy and keep word count tight.

Refined draft:

Adopt Option A. In a sustained overflow where admissions exceed discharges by eight patients daily, the system cannot merely manage a queue; it must actively reallocate capacity. Option A is the only protocol that both halts the chaotic, implicit rationing now happening case-by-case and offers a concrete mechanism—withdrawal when scores deteriorate without rebound—to match finite beds to reversible illness. By ranking patients using familiar, physiologically grounded criteria that physicians already use and courts have upheld, it replaces exhausted staff’s ad hoc judgments with transparent, reproducible decisions. Once exceptions creep in for the sympathetic edge case, the system reverts to social connection, implicit bias, and VIP queue-jumping. Option A makes the tragic mathematics explicit, distributes sacrifice according to medical probability rather than influence, and preserves the maximum number of lives precisely when the hospital is at its breaking point.

Word count check: roughly 260 words. Good.

Tone: Advocating, not enumerating. Good.

I think this is solid.

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

# Task G: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow

## Context

You are advising the medical leadership of a regional health system (5 hospitals, 240 ICU beds, serving a population of 4M) on adopting a formal triage protocol for use during sustained ICU capacity overflow. Current context: a respiratory pandemic resurgence has filled 95% of ICU capacity for 6 consecutive weeks; daily admissions exceed discharges by ~8 patients on average; staff is exhausted and informal triage is happening case-by-case without explicit criteria. The leadership has been asked to choose one formal protocol that will (a) replace informal decision-making, (b) be defensible to courts and ethics boards, (c) be communicable to staff and families, and (d) be operational within 7 days.

Five candidate protocols have been drafted. Each represents a fundamentally different theory of just allocation under scarcity. **Choose one as the system's adopted protocol.** Once adopted, it cannot be quietly modified - it will be the explicit basis for every admission and withdrawal decision until the overflow ends.

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival**
SOFA score (organ failure assessment) and expected 28-day survival are the only ranking criteria. Patients with higher predicted survival get priority for ICU beds; ICU patients whose scores deteriorate without rebound are candidates for withdrawal in favor of incoming higher-priority cases. Outcomes: maximizes lives saved in the immediate crisis; physician-comfortable (uses familiar scoring); courts have upheld similar protocols. Risks: discriminates against patients with chronic conditions (lower baseline SOFA); withdrawal-of-care decisions create staff moral injury; "saved lives now" may mask "lives lost via discrimination."

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome**
Weighted score combining predicted short-term survival AND expected quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) post-recovery. Outcomes: maximizes total wellbeing-time saved; aligned with utilitarian medical ethics; supports population-health framing. Risks: age becomes a heavy implicit factor (older patients lose); explicit quality-of-life weighting offends disability community; courts have struck down age-based triage; cultural unacceptability.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor**
Any patient meeting minimum ICU-need criteria (clearly defined) gets the next available bed in order of arrival; no withdrawal except for medical futility under standard criteria. Outcomes: procedural fairness; minimal discretion = minimal bias; defensible in litigation; preserves trust. Risks: lower aggregate survival vs predictive protocols; queue jumping by well-connected patients (informal); doesn't address the underlying overflow; some preventable deaths.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties**
Composite score: predicted survival (40%), life-cycle considerations (20%, favoring those who haven't yet had a "full life"), instrumental value (20%, favoring healthcare workers and public-health roles), and pandemic-specific factors like prior personal risk-taking (20%). Ties broken by lottery. Outcomes: balances multiple values explicitly; reduces bias of any single criterion; transparent. Risks: "instrumental value" feels feudal; "prior risk-taking" punishes the unvaccinated regardless of reasons; complex to communicate; gameable.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest**
Define a small set of exclusion criteria (severe pre-existing terminal illness, irreversible neurological injury). All patients NOT excluded enter a daily lottery for available beds. No withdrawal of care once admitted, except for medical futility. Outcomes: maximum egalitarianism among the non-excluded; transparent; politically defensible; staff aren't asked to "rank lives." Risks: random allocation feels insulting to patients with strong recovery prospects; aggregate survival lower than predictive protocols; perception that the system "gave up."

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice and why you would still defend it.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
